Flow And Attrition

Goal

This notebook shows how to visualize movement through stages or narrowing through filters.

It adapts the clinical-course flow examples without adding interactive Sankey dependencies.

Analytic Questions

We will ask:

  • How many NHANES respondents remain after common analytic restrictions?
  • Where do observations leave the analysis dataset?
  • How can a simple attrition plot communicate workflow decisions?

Load Data

nhanes_path <- "data/real/nhanes_2021_2023_analysis.csv"

nhanes <- read_csv(nhanes_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

Build Attrition Counts

Each row below is a stage in a simple analysis workflow.

attrition <- tibble::tibble(
  stage = c(
    "All rows",
    "Adults age 18+",
    "Has systolic BP",
    "Has diet summary",
    "Has income-to-poverty"
  ),
  n = c(
    nrow(nhanes),
    nhanes |> filter(age_years >= 18) |> nrow(),
    nhanes |> filter(age_years >= 18, !is.na(systolic_mean)) |> nrow(),
    nhanes |> filter(age_years >= 18, !is.na(systolic_mean), !is.na(mean_daily_energy_kcal)) |> nrow(),
    nhanes |> filter(age_years >= 18, !is.na(systolic_mean), !is.na(mean_daily_energy_kcal), !is.na(family_income_to_poverty)) |> nrow()
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    stage = factor(stage, levels = stage),
    retained_percent = n / first(n) * 100
  )

attrition
# A tibble: 5 × 3
  stage                     n retained_percent
  <fct>                 <int>            <dbl>
1 All rows              11933            100  
2 Adults age 18+         8153             68.3
3 Has systolic BP        6123             51.3
4 Has diet summary       4868             40.8
5 Has income-to-poverty  4303             36.1

Attrition Bars

p1 <- ggplot(attrition, aes(x = stage, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue")

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Rows Retained Through An Analysis Workflow",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of rows"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Add Retention Labels

p1 <- ggplot(attrition, aes(x = stage, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "gray75") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(retained_percent), "% retained")),
    hjust = -0.05,
    size = 3.5
  )

p2 <- p1 +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Analytic Restrictions Change The Available Sample",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of rows"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

p2

Stage-To-Stage Loss

stage_loss <- attrition |>
  mutate(
    previous_n = lag(n),
    lost = previous_n - n
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(lost))

p1 <- ggplot(stage_loss, aes(x = stage, y = lost)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkorange3")

p2 <- p1 +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Rows Lost At Each Step",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Rows lost from previous step"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Takeaways

  • Flow is about movement through stages, not only Sankey diagrams.
  • Attrition plots make analytic restrictions visible.
  • Counts and percentages answer different questions and often belong together.
  • Simple static plots are often enough for analysis workflow communication.
